title: v4_s1_cfg_ft
text:
v4 / fine-tuning


This version fine-tunes v4_s1_logit_t with condition dropout so the sampler can use classifier-free guidance at inference time.






During training, the condition is randomly removed for part of the batch. At inference, conditional and unconditional velocity estimates are combined:
\[
v_{guided}=v_{\emptyset}+w(v_{cond}-v_{\emptyset})
\]
The goal was to push the model away from under-dispersive residuals without retraining the whole architecture.

title: Versions
text:
All experiments start from the same deterministic base: a CNO trained to predict HR - LR on GLORYS surface fields, then fine-tuned with log-absolute temperature-gradient information. The goal of the version tree is to find the best FM model after this CNO: each FM variant learns HR - mu, with mu = LR + CNO(LR). Fine-tuning branches are initialized from v4_s1_logit_t.

Main training and architecture line

v4 · U-Net FM v5 · FM-only v6 · geo physics v7 · Swin local v8 · pixel DiT v9 · diffusion family

v4 ablations

S1 · OT + logit-t S2 · independent S3 · no attention S4 · grad-mu

Fine-tuned from S1

FT · recoarsen FT · CFG FT · regional

v9 branches

v9a · SongUNet EDM v9b · bridge v9c · DiT hybrid






v4

First CNO-conditioned residual Flow Matching family.

v4_s1_logit_t

Best early U-Net FM baseline, using minibatch OT and logit-normal time sampling.

v4_s1_recoarsen_ft

Fine-tuning with re-coarsening consistency.

v4_s1_cfg_ft

Fine-tuning with condition dropout for classifier-free guidance.

v4_s1_regional_ft

Regional oversampling for Mediterranean, Black Sea and polar failures.

v7a_swin

Local window attention using a Swin-style backbone.

v8a_dit_pixel

Pixel-space DiT replacing the U-Net FM backbone.

v9a / v9b / v9c

Diffusion alternatives: EDM, bridge, and DiT hybrid.

title: v9c_dit_cfg_recoarsen
text:
v9c / DiT hybrid


This version combines the v8 DiT backbone with the strongest v4 fine-tuning ideas: condition dropout for CFG and re-coarsening consistency.






The training objective combines FM with coarse consistency:
\[
\mathcal{L}=\mathcal{L}_{FM}+\lambda_c\left\|C(\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR})-\mathbf{x}_{LR}\right\|_2^2.
\]
Condition dropout also makes the model CFG-ready at inference.

title: v9b_songunet_bridge
text:
v9b / diffusion bridge


This version keeps the SongUNet backbone but starts the generative trajectory from a noisy CNO field instead of pure Gaussian noise.






The bridge starts close to the deterministic state:
\[
\mathbf{x}_0=\boldsymbol{\mu}+\sigma(\boldsymbol{\mu})\epsilon,\qquad \mathbf{x}_1=\mathbf{x}_{HR}.
\]
This should keep sampling physically closer to the observed LR-conditioned ocean than a pure noise-to-HR path.

title: v7a_swin
text:
v7 / local transformer


This architecture replaces the U-Net FM backbone with Swin-style local window attention. The question is whether ocean residuals are better modeled by local attention than by global token mixing.






Most energetic submesoscale structures are local: fronts, eddies and coastal gradients. Swin attention restricts attention to windows:
\[
\mathrm{Attn}(Q,K,V)=\mathrm{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right)V
\]
but applies it locally, reducing compute while still letting nearby ocean tokens communicate.

title: v9a_songunet_edm
text:
v9a / EDM diffusion


This version tests a CorrDiff-style diffusion objective with a SongUNet/NCSN++ backbone instead of direct FM velocity prediction.






EDM trains a denoiser over a wide noise range:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{EDM}=\lambda(\sigma)\left\|D_\theta(\mathbf{x}+\sigma\epsilon,\sigma,c)-\mathbf{x}\right\|_2^2.
\]
The goal is to compare a proven diffusion downscaling objective against residual Flow Matching.

title: v4_s1_regional_ft
text:
v4 / fine-tuning


This version keeps the same architecture but changes the sampling distribution. It oversamples regions that were visually weak: Mediterranean, Black Sea and Arctic patches.






Uniform patch sampling gives too much probability mass to open-ocean patches. I changed the patch probability:
\[
p(\text{patch}) \propto w_{region}\,\mathbb{1}(f_{ocean}>\tau)
\]
so small or difficult regions appear more often during fine-tuning.

title: Flow Matching Branch
text:
Flow Matching models the unresolved residual after the CNO mean:
\[
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_{HR} - \boldsymbol{\mu}
\]
The model starts from noise:
\[
\mathbf{x}_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
\]
and learns a velocity field that moves from noise to the residual:
\[
\mathbf{x}_t = (1-t)\mathbf{x}_0 + t\mathbf{x}_1
\]
For the linear path used in the v4 family, the target velocity is:
\[
\mathbf{v}^{*} = \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_0
\]
The training objective is:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{FM} =
\mathbb{E}_{t,\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_1}
\left[
\left\| v_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_t, t, \boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{x}_{LR})
- (\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_0) \right\|_2^2
\right]
\]

title: Flow Matching Branch
text:
Flow Matching models the unresolved residual after the CNO mean:
\[
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_{HR} - \boldsymbol{\mu}
\]
The model starts from noise:
\[
\mathbf{x}_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
\]
and learns a velocity field that moves from noise to the residual:
\[
\mathbf{x}_t = (1-t)\mathbf{x}_0 + t\mathbf{x}_1
\]
For the linear path used in the v4 family, the target velocity is:
\[
\mathbf{v}^{*} = \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_0
\]
The training objective is:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{FM} =
\mathbb{E}_{t,\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_1}
\left[
\left\| v_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_t, t, \boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{x}_{LR})
- (\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_0) \right\|_2^2
\right]
\]

title: Flow Matching Branch
text:
Inference
At inference time the model samples a new residual by solving an ODE:
\[
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = v_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_t, t, \boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{x}_{LR})
\]
The generated field is:
\[
\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \widehat{\mathbf{r}}_{FM}
\]
That is why solver choices matter. Euler, Heun, number of steps, noise temperature and tiling affect the final residual map even when the trained weights are fixed.


Main paper
Flow Matching for Generative Modeling


Useful code reference
g4vrel/CFM


OceanLens implementation
src/oceanlens/losses/fm.py and src/oceanlens/models/fm_unet.py

title: Flow Matching Branch
text:
OceanLens Choices







Choice
Why it matters




Residual target HR - mu
FM only learns what CNO missed


Minibatch OT
Better matching between noise samples and residual targets


Logit-normal t sampling
Samples more useful intermediate times


Heun inference
More accurate ODE integration than Euler


CFG fine-tuning
Allows stronger conditioning at inference


Re-coarsening loss
Keeps generated HR consistent with LR after pooling

title: CNO Branch
text:
The CNO branch is the deterministic part of the decomposition:
\[
\mathbf{x}_{HR} = \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{x}_{LR}) + \mathbf{r}
\]
It estimates the large-scale high-resolution field:
\[
\boldsymbol{\mu} =
\mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
\]
In this project the CNO is trained as a residual super-resolution model:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{CNO} =
\left\| f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
- (\mathbf{x}_{HR} - \mathbf{x}_{LR}) \right\|_2^2
\]
The motivation is pragmatic: CNO should explain the deterministic, stable part of the correction, while the generative model is reserved for the uncertain residual.

title: CNO Branch
text:
The CNO branch is the deterministic part of the decomposition:
\[
\mathbf{x}_{HR} = \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{x}_{LR}) + \mathbf{r}
\]
It estimates the large-scale high-resolution field:
\[
\boldsymbol{\mu} =
\mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
\]
In this project the CNO is trained as a residual super-resolution model:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{CNO} =
\left\| f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
- (\mathbf{x}_{HR} - \mathbf{x}_{LR}) \right\|_2^2
\]
The motivation is pragmatic: CNO should explain the deterministic, stable part of the correction, while the generative model is reserved for the uncertain residual.

title: CNO Branch
text:
Why CNO
CNO is a neural-operator-inspired architecture. The useful idea for OceanLens is that it treats the input and output as spatial fields rather than isolated pixels. That is closer to what ocean downscaling needs: a mapping between functions on a grid.


Paper
Convolutional Neural Operators for robust and accurate learning of PDEs


Official repository
camlab-ethz/ConvolutionalNeuralOperator


OceanLens implementation
src/oceanlens/models/cno.py

title: CNO Branch
text:
Inputs And Outputs



Item
Meaning




Input
Upsampled LR field on the HR grid


Channels
thetao, so, zos, uo, vo


Target
HR - LR


Output
Deterministic residual correction


Final mean
mu = LR + CNO(LR)

title: CNO Branch
text:
Interpretation
If CNO is good, mu should be smooth, stable and physically plausible at large scale. It should not be expected to sample all possible eddy-level details. That is why the second branch exists.

title: Diffusion Bridge
text:
Diffusion Bridge
The bridge variant starts from a noisy CNO/LR-informed state instead of pure Gaussian noise:
\[
\mathbf{x}_0=\boldsymbol{\mu}+\sigma(\boldsymbol{\mu})\epsilon,
\qquad
\mathbf{x}_1=\mathbf{x}_{HR}.
\]
This tests whether a physically meaningful starting point makes the generative path easier than noise-to-HR. In OceanLens this corresponds to v9b.
Related reference: diffusion bridges and stochastic interpolants for downscaling.

title: Logit-normal t Sampling
text:
Logit-normal Time Sampling
Instead of sampling time uniformly, OceanLens tested:
\[
u\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu_t,\sigma_t^2),\qquad t=\sigma(u).
\]
This concentrates training on more informative intermediate states, where the sample is neither pure noise nor already almost the target residual. It is one of the key choices in v4_s1_logit_t.
Reference: Flow Matching.

title: Re-coarsening Loss
text:
Re-coarsening Consistency
The generated HR field should remain compatible with the LR information. The re-coarsening loss applies a coarse operator (C()) to the predicted HR field:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{coarse}=\|C(\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR})-\mathbf{x}_{LR}\|_2^2.
\]
In OceanLens, this was fine-tuned from v4_s1_logit_t to reduce large-scale drift while allowing the FM branch to add small-scale residual energy.
Related idea: consistency losses used in generative downscaling and CorrDiff-style workflows.

title: Minibatch OT
text:
Minibatch Optimal Transport
In the baseline Flow Matching setup, the source noise samples and target residual patches can be paired independently. Minibatch OT improves this by matching noise samples to target residuals with a lower transport cost:
\[
\pi^\star=\arg\min_{\pi}\sum_{i,j}\pi_{ij}\|\mathbf{x}_0^i-\mathbf{x}_1^j\|_2^2.
\]
In OceanLens, this is used in v4_s1_logit_t to make the training path less arbitrary and to expose the U-Net to more coherent transitions between noise and ocean residuals.
Reference: Optimal Transport Conditional Flow Matching.

title: Methods And Architectures
text:
This page is the glossary of the project. It explains the methods and architectures used in OceanLens, why they were tested, and which references motivated them.



Role. Deterministic super-resolution branch. It predicts the stable correction from LR to HR.
\[
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
\]
Why used. It gives a strong deterministic baseline and separates predictable large-scale structure from uncertain high-frequency residuals.
References. Paper · Repository · OceanLens page



Role. Generative residual model. It learns a velocity field from noise to HR - mu.
\[
\mathbf{x}_t = (1-t)\mathbf{x}_0 + t\mathbf{x}_1,
\qquad
\mathbf{v}^{*} = \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_0
\]
Why used. It samples plausible unresolved structures instead of forcing a single deterministic residual.
References. Paper · Reference implementation · OceanLens page



Role. Pairs Gaussian noise samples with residual targets using a small optimal-transport matching inside each batch.
For a batch of noises \(\{\mathbf{x}_0^i\}\) and residual targets \(\{\mathbf{x}_1^j\}\), the matching solves:
\[
\pi^{*} =
\arg\min_{\pi}
\sum_{i,j} \pi_{ij}
\left\| \mathbf{x}_0^i - \mathbf{x}_1^j \right\|_2^2
\]
Why used. It makes the path from noise to target less arbitrary than independent random pairing, which helped make v4_s1_logit_t the strongest early baseline.



Role. Samples more training times around the useful middle of the path instead of treating all t uniformly.
Instead of \(t \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)\), I sample:
\[
u \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2),
\qquad
t = \mathrm{sigmoid}(u)
\]
Why used. The network sees more informative mixed states, where both noise and data structure are present.



Role. Penalizes generated HR if it does not return the LR field after downsampling.
\[
\mathcal{L}_{coarse} =
\left\| C(\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR}) - \mathbf{x}_{LR}^{native} \right\|_2^2
\]
Why used. It is a physical consistency guardrail: the generated fine-scale field should not contradict the coarse observation.



Role. Trains the model with condition dropout, then amplifies the conditional signal at inference:
\[
v_{guided} = v_{\emptyset} + w (v_{cond} - v_{\emptyset})
\]
Why used. It targets the under-dispersive residual problem: FM produced structures in the right places but often with too little amplitude.



Role. Oversamples difficult geographic regions such as the Mediterranean, Black Sea and polar zones.
Patch candidates receive a region-dependent sampling weight:
\[
p(\text{patch}) \propto
w_{region} \cdot \mathbb{1}(\text{ocean fraction} > \tau)
\]
Why used. Uniform patch sampling overrepresents open-ocean regions and underrepresents small enclosed seas and ice-edge regimes.



Role. Local transformer architecture tested in v7a.
Self-attention is restricted to local windows:
\[
\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)
= \mathrm{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right)V
\]
but \(Q,K,V\) only interact inside a spatial window before shifted-window mixing.
Why used. Ocean fronts and mesoscale residuals are often local. Window attention can focus compute on nearby structures instead of spending everything on global attention.
Reference. Swin Transformer



Role. Transformer replacement for the U-Net FM backbone, tested in v8a.
The image is split into tokens, then each block uses attention with time/condition modulation:
\[
\mathbf{h}_{l+1}
= \mathbf{h}_{l}
+ \mathrm{Attn}(\mathrm{AdaLN}(\mathbf{h}_{l}; t, c))
\]
The conditioning \(c\) contains LR/CNO/geographic information depending on the variant.
Why used. Tests whether global token attention and AdaLN-style conditioning help place residual energy better than convolutional bottlenecks.
Reference. Scalable Diffusion Models with Transformers



Role. Diffusion-style denoising architecture tested in v9a.
The denoising objective trains the network to recover the clean target from a noisy state:
\[
\mathbf{y}_{\sigma} = \mathbf{y} + \sigma \boldsymbol{\epsilon},
\qquad
\mathcal{L}_{EDM}
= \lambda(\sigma)
\left\| D_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\sigma}, \sigma, c) - \mathbf{y} \right\|_2^2
\]
Why used. CorrDiff-style downscaling uses strong diffusion backbones and stochastic samplers; this tests a more classical score/diffusion route against FM.
References. EDM paper · NVIDIA PhysicsNeMo



Role. Starts from a noisy CNO/LR-informed state instead of pure Gaussian noise.
Instead of a pure-noise start, the path is conditioned around the coarse estimate:
\[
\mathbf{x}_0 = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sigma(\boldsymbol{\mu})\boldsymbol{\epsilon},
\qquad
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_{HR}
\]
The learned dynamics bridge a physically informed noisy state toward HR.
Why used. The generative path begins closer to the physically conditioned solution, which may reduce useless sampling variance and improve regional stability.



Role. Tests the sampler after training, without changing the weights.
For ODE-based FM inference:
\[
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = v_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t,t,c)
\]
Euler uses one slope per step:
\[
\mathbf{x}_{n+1}=\mathbf{x}_n+\Delta t\,v_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_n,t_n,c)
\]
Heun predicts once, corrects once, then averages both slopes:
\[
\mathbf{x}^{p}_{n+1}=\mathbf{x}_n+\Delta t\,v_n,
\qquad
\mathbf{x}_{n+1}=\mathbf{x}_n+\frac{\Delta t}{2}(v_n+v^{p}_{n+1})
\]
Parameters tested.

Solver: Euler versus Heun.
Steps: fast 20 step runs versus heavier 25/50 step runs.
Noise temperature: sigma=1.0, 1.1, 1.2.
Ensemble size: ens=1 for quick inspection, ens=16/32 for spread.
Tiling: tile_size, tile_overlap, and blending to reduce stitching artifacts.
CFG scale: stronger or weaker conditioning after condition-dropout fine-tuning.

Why used. Some observed failures were not only training failures. Under-dispersive residuals, blue artifacts and horizontal seams can also come from numerical integration, stochastic sampling or patch stitching.

title: Swin Attention
text:
Swin / Window Attention
Swin attention restricts self-attention to local windows and shifts the windows between layers. For ocean residuals, this tests whether local attention is better matched to fronts, eddies and coastal structures than global attention:
\[
\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\mathrm{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right)V.
\]
OceanLens v7a uses this hypothesis: local windows may recover mesoscale patterns with less computation than global DiT attention.
Reference: Swin Transformer.

title: Classifier-free Guidance
text:
Classifier-free Guidance
Condition dropout trains the model to predict both with and without the conditioning. At inference:
\[
v_{guided}=v_\emptyset + w\left(v_c-v_\emptyset\right).
\]
The scale (w) controls how strongly the FM trajectory follows the CNO/LR conditioning. In OceanLens this was tested to fight under-dispersive residuals and increase generated contrast.
Reference: Classifier-Free Diffusion Guidance.

title: Diffusion Transformer
text:
Pixel DiT
The DiT variant replaces the convolutional U-Net with token attention. A patch embedding turns the field into tokens, then time and conditioning modulate transformer blocks, often with AdaLN-style conditioning:
\[
\mathbf{h}_{l+1}=\mathbf{h}_l+\mathrm{Attention}(\mathrm{AdaLN}(\mathbf{h}_l,t,c)).
\]
OceanLens v8a_dit_pixel tests whether global token interactions can better place residual energy in connected ocean regions.
References: DiT, Stable Diffusion 3 / Rectified Flow.

title: EDM / SongUNet
text:
EDM and SongUNet
The EDM-style branch changes the generative objective from direct Flow Matching to denoising across noise levels:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{EDM}=\lambda(\sigma)\|D_\theta(\mathbf{x}+\sigma\epsilon,\sigma,c)-\mathbf{x}\|_2^2.
\]
OceanLens v9a uses this CorrDiff-inspired idea with a Song/NCSN++ U-Net family to test whether diffusion-style preconditioning gives more stable high-frequency residuals.
References: EDM, NCSN++, NVIDIA CorrDiff papers.

title: OceanLens
text:
Residual Decomposition
The project is organized around a Reynolds-like decomposition of the high-resolution ocean state:

xHR = mu(xLR) + runresolved

where the deterministic component is learned by a CNO and the residual component is modeled generatively.


High-resolution state
x_HR
Five surface variables on the GLORYS-like HR grid.


→


Deterministic branch: CNO
mu = x_LR + f_CNO(x_LR)
Learns the stable large-scale correction and produces the conditional mean field.







Generative branch: Flow Matching
r = FM(z, t, mu, x_LR)
Learns the unresolved residual that the deterministic branch cannot recover.

title: OceanLens
text:
Data Construction


01

GLORYS12 HR: 1/12°
Daily surface fields from 1994–2003 are used for training and 2004 for validation. The variables are thetao, so, zos, uo, and vo.



coarsenoffline degraded product


02

Coarse product: 1.5°
The training pipeline reads the already degraded 1.5° product from disk. This is the coarse ocean state before any HR-grid alignment.



interpolatePyTorch nearest


03

LR input: 1/4° grid
The 1.5° field is aligned with the 1/4° tensors using torch.nn.functional.interpolate(mode="nearest"). This gives the model a coarse, HR-shaped condition.



learn


04

Learning targets: HR grid
CNO learns HR - LR. Flow Matching learns HR - mu, where mu = LR + CNO(LR).

title: OceanLens
text:
Training Logic


CNO baseline

LCNO = || fCNO(xLR) - (xHR - xLR) ||22

The CNO is the deterministic baseline. Its output is added to LR to form:

mu = xLR + fCNO(xLR)



Flow Matching residual

x1 = xHR - mu ; x0 ~ N(0, I)

FM learns a velocity field along the path from noise to residual:

xt = (1 - t)x0 + tx1 ; v* = x1 - x0

title: OceanLens
text:
Inference Experiments
Inference has two stages. First, CNO gives a deterministic estimate:

mu = xLR + fCNO(xLR)

Then FM samples the residual by integrating a learned velocity field from noise to the residual space:

dxt/dt = vFM(xt, t, mu, xLR) ; x̂HR = mu + xt=1


Initial noise: sigma ODE solver: Euler / Heun Steps: 20 / 25 / 50 Conditioning: CFG scale Sampling: ensemble Map stitching: tile + overlap

title: v5_fm_only
text:
v5 / FM-only


This version removes the deterministic CNO branch from the generative path and tests how far Flow Matching can go without the strong CNO mean field. It is a stress test: if FM-only fails, it confirms that the CNO decomposition is necessary.






The v4 setup decomposes:
\[
\mathbf{x}_{HR}=\boldsymbol{\mu}+\mathbf{r}.
\]
v5 asks whether the generative model can learn the missing field with weaker deterministic support. This is useful as a baseline against the CNO-conditioned FM family.

title: v4_s1_recoarsen_ft
text:
v4 / fine-tuning


This version fine-tunes v4_s1_logit_t with a coarse-scale consistency constraint. The objective is to keep the generated HR field compatible with the LR observation after downsampling.






The generated field is:
\[
\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \widehat{\mathbf{r}}_{FM}
\]
I add a re-coarsening loss:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{coarse}
= \left\| C(\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR}) - \mathbf{x}_{LR}^{native} \right\|_2^2
\]
where C is the mask-aware downsampling operator used in the training system with PyTorch average pooling.

title: v6_full_geo_phys
text:
v6


This version tests whether explicit geographic and physical context can reduce the regional failures observed near coasts, polar zones and semi-enclosed seas.






The baseline residual model only sees dynamic ocean fields. v6 adds stronger geographic/static context so the model can condition residual generation on where it is in the ocean:
\[
v_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t,t,c),
\qquad
c = [\boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{x}_{LR}, \mathrm{mask}, \mathrm{geo}]
\]
The intention is to make the residual less blind to coastal and polar regimes.

title: v4_s2_independent
text:
v4 / S2


This ablation keeps the same CNO-conditioned U-Net FM as S1, but removes minibatch OT. Noise and residual targets are paired independently, which tests whether optimal pairing is actually helping residual structure.






The residual target is unchanged:
\[

\]
The only change is the coupling:
\[
(\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_1)\sim p_0(\mathbf{x}_0)p_1(\mathbf{x}_1)
\]
instead of solving the minibatch OT assignment.

\]
instead of only using the coarse/deterministic fields. If FM needs front locations to place residual energy, this variant should improve coastal and current-front structure.

title: v4_s1_logit_t
text:
v4 / S1


This is the strongest early residual Flow Matching baseline. I kept the CNO-conditioned U-Net FM setup, but changed the coupling and time sampling so the model sees more informative training pairs and more useful intermediate times.






The deterministic CNO gives:
\[
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
\]
The FM model learns only the missing residual:
\[
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_{HR} - \boldsymbol{\mu}
\]
For S1, I use minibatch OT to pair noise and targets:
\[
\pi^{*}=\arg\min_{\pi}\sum_{i,j}\pi_{ij}
\left\|\mathbf{x}_0^i-\mathbf{x}_1^j\right\|_2^2
\]
and logit-normal time sampling:
\[
u \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t,\sigma_t^2),
\qquad
t=\mathrm{sigmoid}(u)
\]
The goal was to improve the training signal around the mixed states where both noise and ocean structure are present.

title: v8a_dit_pixel
text:
v8 / pixel DiT


This version replaces the U-Net FM backbone with a pixel-space Diffusion Transformer. It tests whether token attention can recover structures that the convolutional U-Net misses.






The HR patch is patchified into tokens. Time and conditioning modulate the transformer through AdaLN-style layers:
\[
\mathrm{block}(z,t,c)=z+\gamma(t,c)\,\mathrm{Attn}(\mathrm{LN}(z))+\beta(t,c).
\]
The goal is to test global context for residual energy placement, especially in regions where local tiling produced horizontal artifacts. The figures currently shown use the best local copy available: Heun 50, sigma=1.0, ensemble=1, tile=512, overlap=256.

title: v4_s3_no_attn
text:
v4 / S3


This ablation removes the bottleneck attention from the v4 U-Net FM. It tests whether attention is needed to place the residual energy, or whether convolution alone is enough.






The FM objective is unchanged:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{FM}=\left\|v_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t,t,\boldsymbol{\mu},\mathbf{x}_{LR})-(\mathbf{x}_1-\mathbf{x}_0)\right\|_2^2.
\]
Only the attention block is removed. If the maps lose coherent fronts or eddy placement, the bottleneck attention was contributing useful spatial coordination.
